title: Data Processing Principles
intent: Personal data must be processed lawfully, fairly, and transparently. Controllers should limit collection to what is necessary (data minimization), use data only for stated purposes (purpose limitation), keep data accurate, store for limited periods (storage limitation), and ensure integrity, confidentiality and accountability for processing activities.
triggers: we only collect what we need
we will protect your personal data
we will not keep your data longer than
examples: we only collect the minimum information necessary to provide the service
data is used solely to improve our product
we retain account data for 12 months after inactivity
we take reasonable steps to ensure data accuracy
access controls and logging are used to protect user data
we do not use personal data for unrelated purposes

title: Lawful Basis
intent: Processing of personal data must rely on at least one lawful basis: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests. Policies should state which basis applies for each processing purpose and, if relying on legitimate interests, explain the balancing test.
triggers: necessary for the performance of a contract
to comply with a legal obligation
we process based on our legitimate interests
examples: we process your payment information to fulfil orders (contract)
we process user data because you agreed (consent)
we keep records to comply with tax law (legal obligation)
we analyze usage metrics based on our legitimate interests to improve UX

title: Conditions for Consent
intent: Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. Controllers must enable easy withdrawal and must not use pre-ticked boxes or bundled consent. Record-keeping of consent is required where consent is the basis for processing.
examples: users must check an unchecked box to consent to marketing
you may withdraw consent at any time via account settings
we ask for explicit consent before processing health data
consent is recorded with timestamp and scope

title: Children's Data
intent: Processing of personal data of children under the age set by Member States (default under GDPR: under 16, but possibly lowered to 13) requires parental/guardian consent. Policies must specify age rules and parental consent mechanisms where applicable.
examples: we require parental consent for users under 16
age verification is performed during signup
content intended for minors requires explicit parental permission

title: Transparent Information and Modalities
intent: Information provided to data subjects must be concise, transparent, intelligible, and easily accessible, using clear and plain language. Information should be readily available and tailored for different contexts (e.g., short notices, detailed policy).
examples: a short privacy notice on signup with link to full policy
FAQ explaining user rights in plain language
privacy settings presented in clear, non-technical terms

title: Information at Time of Direct Collection
intent: When personal data is collected directly from the data subject, the controller must provide, at the time of collection, a privacy notice that includes the controller's identity, contact details, purposes of processing, lawful basis, recipients, retention periods, rights (access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection), and the existence of automated decision-making if applicable.
triggers: at the time when personal data are obtained
provide the data subject with the following information
examples: privacy notice displayed on a signup form
in-app notification about data collection on first use
cookie banner linking to detailed privacy policy
we collect your IP address to maintain security
we use Google Analytics to measure traffic

title: Information When Data Not Obtained from Subject
intent: If personal data is obtained from sources other than the subject, the controller must provide the subject with information about the source, categories of data, purposes of processing, recipients, retention period, and rights, within a reasonable time.
triggers: data not directly collected from you
we enrich profiles using third-party sources
we receive personal data from vendors
examples: we receive transaction history from payment processors
we enrich user profiles with data from our partners
we may acquire contact lists from third-party providers

title: Right of Access
intent: Data subjects have the right to obtain confirmation of whether their personal data is being processed and, where so, access to that data and certain information about processing. Policies should describe the process and contact for access requests (DSARs).
triggers: request a copy of your data
you can request information about your data
examples: users can submit a DSAR via privacy@company.com
we provide a copy of personal data in response to access requests within statutory timeframe
a web form for requesting account data

title: Right to Rectification
intent: Data subjects have the right to obtain rectification of inaccurate personal data concerning them. Policies should explain how to request corrections and how requests are handled.
examples: users can update their address and contact details from account settings
contact privacy@company.com to request correction

title: Right to Erasure (Right to be Forgotten)
intent: Data subjects can request erasure of personal data where it is no longer necessary, consent is withdrawn, processing is unlawful, or compliance with a legal obligation is required. Policies should describe erasure procedures and any limitations (e.g., legal retention obligations).
triggers: we will erase your data upon request
examples: users can request account deletion and data erasure
we will delete personal data except where retained for legal obligations

title: Right to Restrict Processing
intent: Individuals can obtain restriction of processing in specific cases (e.g., contesting accuracy, unlawful processing, objections pending resolution). Controllers must inform data subjects when restriction is lifted.
examples: user requests that their account data not be processed while accuracy is verified
we suspend use of data pending investigation

title: Notification Obligation Regarding Rectification or Erasure
intent: When personal data is rectified or erased, or processing restricted, controllers must communicate the rectification, erasure or restriction to each recipient to whom the data has been disclosed, unless impossible or disproportionate.
examples: we will notify our analytics vendors if data is erased
we will inform integrations to stop processing the deleted account

title: Right to Data Portability
intent: Data subjects have the right to receive personal data they provided in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format, and to transmit it to another controller where processing is based on consent or contract and carried out by automated means.
examples: download account data in CSV or JSON
export contacts for transfer to another service

title: Right to Object
intent: Individuals can object to processing based on public interest or legitimate interests, including profiling; they can also object to processing for direct marketing. Controllers must stop processing unless compelling legitimate grounds prevail or for legal claims.
examples: users can opt out of targeted advertising
a clear unsubscribe link in marketing emails
process halted when objection is raised unless necessary

title: Automated Individual Decision-Making & Profiling
intent: Individuals have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produce legal effects or similarly significant effects. Policies should disclose profiling practices and safeguards including human intervention and ability to contest decisions.
triggers: you may be subject to automated decisions
examples: automated credit scoring used to approve loans
automated suitability scoring for job applications
profile-based personalized pricing

title: Security of Processing
intent: Controllers and processors must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk: encryption, pseudonymisation, access controls, resilience, regular testing, and measures to restore availability and access following incidents.
examples: we use TLS encryption for data in transit
personal data is encrypted at rest
two-factor authentication for admin access
regular vulnerability scanning and penetration testing
we have a data breach incident response plan

title: General Principle for Transfers (Chapter V)
intent: Cross-border transfers of personal data to third countries or international organisations may only occur if the conditions set out in Chapter V are met (adequacy, safeguards, or derogations). Policies should indicate when and how data is transferred internationally.
triggers: we may process data in servers located in
examples: your data may be processed on servers located in the US
we may transfer certain information to third-party service providers in other countries

title: Transfers on the Basis of an Adequacy Decision
intent: Transfers are permitted where the European Commission has decided that a third country or territory ensures an adequate level of protection. Policies should reference adequacy decisions where applicable.
triggers: data transfer to a country with adequacy
examples: we transfer data to countries benefiting from an adequacy decision
transfer to Canada (subject to adequacy) for certain data

title: Appropriate Safeguards for Transfers
intent: In absence of an adequacy decision, transfers require appropriate safeguards such as Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Binding Corporate Rules, codes of conduct, or approved contractual mechanisms. Policies should disclose the safeguards used for transfers.
examples: we rely on EU Standard Contractual Clauses with our US processors
we have binding corporate rules approved by data protection authorities

title: Binding Corporate Rules
intent: Groups may transfer data within the corporate group using Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) approved by competent supervisory authorities; policies should reference BCRs where used and their approval status.
examples: we transfer data between group companies under approved BCRs
our BCRs ensure enforceable data subject rights across group

title: Transfers or Disclosures Not Authorized by Union Law
intent: Transfers or disclosures to a third country or international organization on the basis of international agreements or request by public authorities are permitted only where compatible with EU law; policies should be cautious about compliance with foreign legal orders and describe response to such requests.
examples: we may be required to disclose information in response to a lawful request from a foreign court
we evaluate legal requests from non-EU authorities against EU law

title: Derogations for Specific Situations
intent: In the absence of adequacy or safeguards, occasional transfers may be permitted under specific derogations, e.g., explicit consent from the data subject, performance of a contract, important public interest, or legal claims; policies should identify any reliance on derogations and the scope.
examples: we may transfer data outside the EU where you have given explicit consent
transfer required to fulfil a contract with an overseas provider
